questions.question: What link does Mixture of Experts break?
options: The link between data and parameters

Dense models pay FLOPs equal to parameter count on EVERY token — MoE replaces each FFN with E experts plus a router picking k per token, so storage scales with E while compute scales with k (e.g., DeepSeek-V3: 671B total, 37B active)
The link between loss and gradients
The link between layers and depth
questions.explanation: Typical 2026 config: E=256, k=8. The top 10 open models on Artificial Analysis are all MoE — sparsity is the scaling idea of the decade.

questions.question: What does an MoE block look like mechanically?
options: E parallel attention layers voting
Attention is unchanged; the FFN is swapped: a single linear router scores E experts per token, the top-k are selected, and the output is a gated mixture of those k independent FFNs (typically SwiGLU)
Experts process different documents
A tree of routers at every layer
questions.explanation: Each token picks its own experts. The router is just one linear layer — the intelligence is in what the gradient teaches the experts to specialize in.

questions.question: What is the load-balancing problem?
options: GPUs overheating unevenly

If the router sends 90% of tokens to one expert, the rest starve and capacity is wasted — classic fixes are auxiliary balance losses (Switch, Mixtral) or capacity limits with token dropping, both with costs
Tokens arriving out of order
Experts having different sizes
questions.explanation: The auxiliary loss adds a hyperparameter and a competing gradient; capacity limits drop tokens and hurt quality — which set up 2024's better answer.

questions.question: How does DeepSeek-V3's auxiliary-loss-free balancing work?
options: It removes the router entirely

A learned per-expert BIAS shifts top-k selection and is nudged ±γ outside the training loss based on each expert's usage vs target — while gating probabilities use the raw scores unchanged, decoupling routing from expression
It trains each expert on its own GPU
It randomizes expert assignment
questions.explanation: No penalty pollutes the main objective: balance is enforced by selection-time bias only. The lesson calls it 2024's big unlock.

questions.question: Why does MoE dominate the 2026 frontier?
options: MoE models are easier to fine-tune

Dense scaling hit a wall — meaningfully smarter meant exponentially more FLOPs per token; MoE decouples capacity from per-token compute, so a 671B/37B-active model beats a dense 70B at comparable inference cost
Regulators capped dense model sizes
MoE requires no load balancing
questions.explanation: DeepSeek-V3, Mixtral, Qwen2.5-MoE, Llama 4, Kimi K2, gpt-oss — the architecture answer to the compute wall is to stop paying for parameters you don't use per token.
